story:
Rhonda Wagnar walks into a smoke-thick speakeasy with a bloody string of pearls at her throat and a Colt in her fist. Sequins glitter underfoot. The city's most powerful man, Russell Rossi—boss of the family that buys politicians, rents out the law, and grips every business in Chicago—waits at the center table. Rhonda is his right hand and his hidden affair, dressed in black as if mourning the girl she used to be. Once he sent flowers; now he sends her to do his violent errands.

She takes a drag, steadies her heart, and says it straight: the mark got away. A glass flies past her head and explodes; the room empties in a rush of chairs. Rossi rises, all cold muscle and old eyes, grabs her jaw, and demands to know how she could fail after weeks of planning. She doesn't flinch. She says a detective from the Fifth District shadowed her all night, like someone tipped the cops to the job. In the scramble, she fired on the tail to break free. From the garter at her thigh, she produces a blood-smeared scrap of cloth, scrawled with three clues: "Rossi. Plan? Chase. Women agents?" Proof, she says, that the police knew too much, and that the leak runs close.

Rossi paces, drinking fast, shirt buttons askew, fury barely leashed. He orders her to find the missing man while he hunts the traitor who put the law on their heels. “I know those pearls bite your skin like tokens of bruises and lies,” he says. Then, with a flick of his hand, he dismisses her like a problem he'll deal with later. The air between them tightens—history and habit, longing and harm—but Rhonda lets the softness in her eyes harden again. The night goddess returns.

Outside, her Rolls waits. She slips behind the wheel. A man sits in the passenger seat, smile sharp, an eagle-topped badge gleaming at his lapel. He asks whether Rossi pressed her. Only the usual, she tells him. Good girl, he murmurs. Their kiss is quick, urgent, then she drops the car into gear and aims for open road, city lights shrinking in the rearview.

Only then does the picture come into focus: the "botched" hit was a play. Rhonda steered Rossi's fury toward a phantom leak while she slipped the mark out from under the family's hand. She's not his weapon, not his secret anymore. The pearls at her throat bite into skin—tokens of a life that paid in bruises and lies—so she yanks hard. The strand snaps. White beads, stained red, scatter into the dark and are gone.

Chicago remains under Rossi's thumb, for now. He will look for a betrayer and find shadows. Rhonda will have to stay quicker than rumor, truer than fear. But tonight she has put distance between herself and the man who used her grief and hunger. She has chosen a side that asks her to protect instead of destroy. The smoke thins. The road opens. The woman in black leaves the city that taught her how to hurt—and learns how to stop.
